pattern: Products swing wildly with sign changes, so the winning subset is found by reasoning about how many negative numbers you keep and whether a lone zero beats an unavoidable negative, not by sorting values naively and taking the largest ones.
hints: Think about what a product does when you multiply in a negative number -- sometimes two negatives kept together beat leaving both of them out, even though each one alone would shrink the product.

Because there are at most 13 numbers, you can afford to enumerate every one of the (at most 2^13 - 1) non-empty subsets directly and keep a running best product -- for this size limit that is a completely legitimate strategy, not just a fallback.

If you want the closed-form version instead: take every positive number, take negatives in pairs from most-negative to least-negative (an even count of negatives always helps), and if you are left with exactly one unpaired negative and nothing else usable, compare against picking a single zero or a single leftover value instead.

edge_inputs.description: mixed positives and negatives, several profitable pairings available
all negative with an odd count, one must be excluded to leave an even, positive-product pairing
single positive element, nothing else to choose from
single negative element must still be chosen since the subset cannot be empty
maximum length, thirteen identical maximum-value entries
a zero next to an unpaired negative -- isolating the zero beats keeping the negative
even count of negatives -- keeping every one of them (not excluding any) gives the largest product
zeros mixed with positives -- the zeros should simply be ignored

stress.description: random small integers across the full value range at random lengths up to the constraint ceiling
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 13)
    nums = [rng.randint(-9, 9) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
